macro_segments.title: The Mishna's Case of Three Brothers and the Question of Zika

micro_segments.title: Setup of the Mishna: Three brothers, two married to sisters, one to a third woman
The sequence of divorce and death: Leah is divorced, then Levi dies, then Shimon marries Devorah
The Gemara's inference: The case implies Yesh Zika even with two brothers
Rav Nechman's challenge: He holds Ein Zika, so how does the Mishna exclude cases?

macro_segments.title: Reconciling Yesh Zika and Ein Zika Through Different Theories of Tzarat Erva

micro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's response: The case would still be problematic without ma'amar
The role of ma'amar: It signals that Rachel is pulled in, but not that it creates the problem
Beit Shammai's position: Ma'amar creates full acquisition; Rabbis disagree
Rav Nechman's position explained: Rachel is only a problem because of ma'amar, not zika alone

macro_segments.title: Three Scenarios of Marriage and Divorce: Which Creates Tzarat Erva?

micro_segments.title: Scenario A: Leah divorced before Levi dies
Scenario B: Levi dies, then Leah divorced
Scenario C: Shimon marries Devorah, then divorces Leah before his death
The debate: Does tzarat erva depend on nisuin rishonim or mita mapelet?

macro_segments.title: Two Interpretations: Rabbi Yirmiyah (Nisuin Rishonim) and Rava (Mita Mapelet)

micro_segments.title: If we hold like Rabbi Yirmiyah: They cannot be married simultaneously
What the Mishna excludes under Rabbi Yirmiyah: The case of married then divorced
If we hold like Rava: Go by time of death, so divorce anytime before death works
Rava's conclusion: If Yesh Zika, the Mishna excludes only the case of no divorce at all

macro_segments.title: Safek Kiddushin and Safek Gerushin: The Status Quo Principle

micro_segments.title: The new topic: Cases where the woman's status is uncertain—married or divorced
The principle: In safek, the woman does chalitza, not yibum
Safek Kiddushin: The ring falls between them; we don't know whose domain it entered
Safek Gerushin: Three defective get cases—handwriting only, no witnesses, no date

macro_segments.title: Why Safek Gerushin Doesn't Follow the Parallel of Safek Kiddushin

micro_segments.title: The question: Why doesn't the Mishna give the case of a safek get that fell in her domain?
Rabba's principle: Status quo—she was married, so assume she still is
Applying status quo to kiddushin: She was unmarried, so she remains unmarried
The Gemara's challenge: If status quo applies, don't demand chalitza in either case

macro_segments.title: The Cost of Chumra: Why Safek Kiddushin Demands Chalitza but Safek Gerushin Doesn't

micro_segments.title: The concern in safek kiddushin: If we don't allow yibum, people think Rachel is married
Why chalitza is a no-cost chumra here: Chalitza itself shows Rachel is out of the picture
Safek gerushin is different: Chalitza sends the wrong message that Rachel is divorced
The Gemara's resolution: In safek gerushin, don't demand chalitza to avoid misleading message

macro_segments.title: The Case of the Fallen House: Applying Status Quo and Message Logic to Safek Death

micro_segments.title: The scenario: A house falls on Shimon and Rachel; we don't know who died first
The status quo issue: Devorah was always permitted to do yibum with Reuven
Why chalitza is demanded despite status quo: The case is rare, so the cost is minimal
Contrast with safek gerushin: Divorce is common, so demanding chalitza carries real risk
The concern: In safek gerushin, people will think Rabbis verified Rachel's divorce, leading to improper yibum

topical_tags.term: Nisuin Rishonim (marriage status determines tzarat ervah)
Chalitza (removal of shoe in yibum waiver)
